title: Bear country need not be  a death trap for runners
summary: I have a confession; I've been running in bear country again. I know, a bunch of people who study bears say this is bad. We heard it again when runner Michael Mungoven stumbled into a sow grizzly near Anchor Point at the end of May and got chewed up. He survived, as do most bear attack victims, but still there were the warnings: Don't run in bear country. You'll surprise a bear. The bear will attack you. You could be killed. Yes this could happen. Lots of things could happen. The Earth could be struck by an asteroid killing us all while we are safely locked in our homes watching TV and waiting for heart disease to kill us. But let's be real for a minute. According to David Ropeik, the director of risk communication at the Harvard School of Public Health, and George Gray -- who together wro…

title: Tagged halibut await anglers when Homer derby starts Monday
summary: ON THE COOK INLET WHALE BITE -- With nine halibut caught, tagged and gently released back into the cold waters of Cook Inlet to await the May start of the Homer Jackpot Halibut Derby, captain Pete Wedin leaned back against the rail of the Julia Lynn and captured the essence of the day. "Now I remember why I don't like to fish halibut in April,'' he said. "My fingers are cold.'' Snow lashed across the aft deck. The Julia Lynn, caught between a brisk wind and an outrushing current, rolled aggressively on her beam between incoming Gulf of Alaska swells. From the comfort of the heated cabin, the VHF radio crackled with weather comments from the skipper of another tagging boat: "Now we've got some sleet with the mix.'' On the public broadcast radio, a Kenai Peninsula radio station blared out "W…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: CRAIG MEDRED Fine work by troopers gives lesson in fishing CRAIG MEDRED OUTDOORS Published: June 25, 2006 Last Modified: June 25, 2006 at 02:40 AM RUSSIAN RIVER -- I got a ticket Monday morning, and the Alaska State Troopers who wrote it deserve a compliment. They served up a costly reminder that in Alaska fisheries there is a daily bag limit -- not a daily take-home limit. Once you've taken your catch for the day, it doesn't matter if someone steals the fish, a bear grabs them or you simply lose some. They're still your fish. A bad decision to just sort of overlook this rule put me in the uncomfortable position of trying to explain to a couple of state troopers what happened to a missing fish. Though there were only the fillets from a daily bag limit of three red salmon in my possession, …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: CRAIG MEDRED Small streams, fast boats a bad mix Published: June 4, 2006 Last Modified: June 4, 2006 at 04:13 AM There were strange dreams of Kevin Delaney as the winds whispered in the night and geese honked in the dark. But then, Kevin had been on my mind for days. Once the director of sport fisheries for the Alaska Department of Fish and Game, he grew tired of the work, got frustrated with the difficulty of moving the system and finally turned his back on it all, abandoning a career in conservation for a shot at getting rich in the stock market. Maybe it was partly my own midlife struggles that had me thinking about Kevin. But mainly, I think, it was remnants of work from the previous week. Some time had been spent researching Alaska boating deaths. The reporting brought back memories o…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: CRAIG MEDRED Little Su becoming a victim of those who love it Published: May 28, 2006 Last Modified: May 28, 2006 at 03:30 AM LITTLE SUSITNA RIVER -- All it took was the pounding wake from one, big, inboard-powered jet boat to turn the water from the color of weak tea to the constituency of a sand slurry. If a placer miner did this to an Alaska stream, the state Department of Environmental Conservation or the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency would look to prosecute. The damage that turbidity does to aquatic productivity in Alaska salmon streams is, after all, well documented. Somehow, though, everyone overlooks that when a stream gets polluted in the name of pleasure. Fifteen years ago, when the state was designating important recreational rivers in the Susitna Valley, there was talk o…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Clams digging in Story by CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: June 4, 2006 Last Modified: June 5, 2006 at 04:11 AM CLAM GULCH -- Clamming is the perfect combination of fishing, hunting and beachcombing. An ideal family activity, there is something here for everyone: • For adults, tasty shellfish. • For the kids, a chance to splash in the water and dig in the mud. • For ages young and old, the fun of the chase, or at least what qualifies as the chase on the beaches of Cook Inlet when the tide is out. To find clams, a clammer scurries around looking for dimples in the otherwise smooth surface of the sand. When a dimple is found, some quick digging is required on the ocean side to get at the clam that will at the same time be digging deeper into the beach to escape. Catching them is …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Rangers search for 2 climbers MOUNT FORAKER: Ripped backpack found 300 feet off route. By PETER PORCO Anchorage Daily News Published: June 3, 2006 Last Modified: June 3, 2006 at 02:35 AM Denali National Park rangers planned to resume a search today for two people overdue from a climb on Mount Foraker in the Alaska Range. Helicopter searches of the climbers' intended route Thursday night and Friday, hampered by cloudy weather, failed to find them. However, the searchers did find and retrieve a backpack belonging to one of the climbers, said Daryl Miller, the park's South District ranger. The pack was torn and contained a few items, Miller said from his office in Talkeetna. Miller declined to identify either of the climbers or their hometowns. "We're still trying to figure all this out, and …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: With winter's end, Juneau anglers are looking forward to fly-fishing ACTION: Season starts at end of April and runs to first week of October. By ERIC MORRISON The Juneau Empire Published: May 21, 2006 Last Modified: May 21, 2006 at 04:39 AM JUNEAU -- Winter is officially over for some of Juneau's most dedicated fly-fishermen. The Raincountry Flyfishers club held its annual year-end casting clinic recently at Twin Lakes to iron out some kinks and get ready for the many angling options Juneau offers during the summer. The club meets twice a week from October to April for instructive meetings and fly-tying sessions. "This is the last session we have before the summer," said president Mark Vinsel. "Everybody would much rather be fishing when the fish are in and the weather is nicer." Vinsel sa…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fishing hole is no place to share life's details on cell phone RON WILMOT COMMENT Published: June 11, 2006 Last Modified: June 11, 2006 at 03:40 AM Cell phones have become such a part of 21st century life that it is passe to complain about their use in public. But in any situation where people are standing around waiting for something, you are bound to hear people blithely blab on about personal details that would be embarrassing to many folks. Apparently, people have little to hide these days. MySpace is evidence of that. Or maybe they want everyone to know. I don't want to know. Not long ago, bringing your cell phone and jabbering away at a fishing hole was tantamount to farting at a funeral. Recently, churches in Mexico had to resort to using jamming devices to keep the cell phones of p…

title: 1 of 1 Above: Members of a Marine honor guard from Echo Company, 4th Reconnaissance Battalion, Elmendorf Air Force Base,…
summary: 1 of 1 Above: Members of a Marine honor guard from Echo Company, 4th Reconnaissance Battalion, Elmendorf Air Force Base, lower their weapons Wednesday after firing a salute during a memorial service for Brendan Smart at the Mile 16 parking lot in Hatcher Pass. Smart, 24, died in an avalanche on Marmot Mountain on Feb. 28. He was an Iraq war veteran who served in the Marines as a heavy-equipment operator from 2002 to 2004. Story, Page B-2 At left : Marine Staff Sgt. Robert Dela Rosa from Echo Company salutes after presenting Brenda Smart with a flag during a memorial service for her son, Brendan. Photos by STEPHEN NOWERS ANCHORAGE DAILY NEWS Stephen Nowers

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Snowboarder recalled with tears and joy AVALANCHE: The Iraq vet was buried in snow on a Hatcher Pass run. By ANDREW WELLNER Anchorage Daily News Published: June 22, 2006 Last Modified: June 22, 2006 at 09:47 AM HATCHER PASS -- Family and friends gathered Wednesday to remember Brendan Smart, the Anchorage snowboarder killed February in an avalanche, in the place where it happened. A U.S. Marine Corps honor guard from Elmendorf Air Force Base's reserve battalion stood on a hill above the last parking lot before the Hatcher Pass Lodge, played taps and offered a tribute to Smart, an Iraq war veteran who served in the Marines as a heavy-equipment operator from 2002 to 2004. Pastor Tracy Simmons of Christ Community Church in Anchorage and a friend of Brendan's mother, Brenda Smart, gave the eulo…

title: Slam'n Salm'n for charity
summary: Vincent "Vinny" Eben waited out the final minutes of the Ship Creek king salmon derby in his black Chevy pickup, glancing at a crumpled thank-you speech he was hoping he'd get to read at the closing ceremony Sunday. He had caught his 39.4-pound fish a week ago. Fortunately for him no one did better in what many called a painfully slow fishing derby. Right after 3 p.m. Sunday, closing time for the 11-day Ship Creek Slam'n Salm'n Derby, organizer Marty Turnbow bee-lined to Eben's truck and shook his hand. Eben won $1,000 cash and a trip to Ultima Thule Lodge in Alaska's Wrangell Mountains for catching the overall biggest fish in the derby. Many complained that the derby preceded the fish this year. Biologists have said cooler weather and water kept salmon in the salt water longer. This year,…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Eben clings to lead Alaska sports digest Published: June 10, 2006 Last Modified: June 10, 2006 at 03:09 AM FISHING Eben clings to lead On a cloudy, windy day, nothing changed atop the leader board of the Downtown Soup Kitchen's Slam'n Salm'n Derby at Ship Creek. Anchorage Vincent Eben, who landed a 39.4-pounder, is now less than 48 hours away from winning. The derby ends 3 p.m. Sunday. Friday's mystery weight was 31.80 pounds and Stephen Gebert weighed a 26.85-pounder, closest without going over, to win a $100 gift certificate. Today, derby ticket holders can win $300 in a random drawing sponsored by Tesoro 2 Go. Downtown Soup Kitchen's Slam'n Salm'n Derby At Ship Creek Through 8:30 p.m. Friday Pro Class -- 1) Vincent Eben, Anchorage, 39.40 pounds; 2) Ed Taylor, Anchorage, 37.20; 3) Mel Sm…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Kings slowly rolling into Deshka With Tropical Storm Alberto headed for Florida this week to kick off the hurricane season, Orlando couple Sis and Tom Myers figured they would head north and trade the big blow for Alaska's best season -- fishing season. (Updated: June 15, 2006, 3:50 AM) ‹ Fishing chart ‹ Fishing on the Deshka photo gallery Kenai becomes a combat zone On the Sterling Highway beside the granite-green Kenai River, parades of giant recreational vehicles rolled on Tuesday. Two of Alaska's most popular rivers -- the Kenai and the Russian -- open here Sunday at 12:01 a.m. for reds, rainbow trout and Dolly Varden. (Updated: June 8, 2006, 3:27 AM) High water sinks Ship Creek catch rate Day after day of beautiful spring sunshine lured Anchorage's Mike Rhodes into fishing for kings o…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fishing chart Published: June 15, 2006 Last Modified: June 15, 2006 at 03:50 AM SUSITNA VALLEY Matanuska Valley lakes Trout, grayling, char, landlocked salmon, pike ***** Fishing remains good to excellent; lakes have been recently stocked. Check out Seventeenmile and Kepler-Bradley for trout -- Finger or Knik lakes for grayling. Talkeetna area lakes Trout, grayling, char **** Trout fishing remains good, though warm days will start taking the edge off midday action. Try X, Y and north and south Friend lakes. Little Susitna River (1) King salmon, trout **** King fishing has picked up and anglers were catching some lunkers midweek. Dozens were caught on Sunday, mainly from boats. Try large plugs and Spin-N-Glos. Montana Creek (2) Trout, grayling, kings **** For the past two days, Montana Cree…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Sportsman has vacation to remember HUNT, FISH: North Pole man shot his first brown bear, took lead in Valdez Halibut Derby. By TIM MOWRY Fairbanks Daily News-Miner Published: June 11, 2006 Last Modified: June 11, 2006 at 03:38 AM FAIRBANKS -- If Rich Piech had another week or two of vacation, who knows what he might have accomplished? Maybe he'd have caught a 50-pound king salmon or shot a big black bear -- or both. Piech of North Pole could have bagged an Alaska triple crown or grand slam, if you will. As it was, Piech had a pretty decent two weeks, even by Alaska standards. After shooting his first brown bear, a 7-foot-plus grizzly in Kamishak Bay on May 24, Piech reeled in a 195-pound halibut to take the lead in the Valdez Halibut Derby. "It's been a pretty good two weeks," said Piech, …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Kenai king anglers can use bait Saturday By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: June 9, 2006 Last Modified: June 9, 2006 at 03:11 PM As a late spring finally blossoms into an Alaska summer, state fisheries biologists are finding that what they projected about regional salmon runs is proving true. Fish are returning in ample numbers, but they hesitated before entering spawning streams because of cool water temperatures. All that is changing now. With king salmon flooding into the Kenai River, the Alaska Department of Fish and Game announced Friday it will allow fishing with bait in the river downstream from Moose River starting today (SATURDAY). Bait is far and away the most effective way to catch kings in the turbid Kenai. Fish and Game thinks allowing bait will keep thousands of …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Photographers go Wild An Alaska contest showcases some of the world's top nature images By MELISSA DeVAUGHN Anchorage Daily News Published: June 11, 2006 Last Modified: June 11, 2006 at 03:35 AM Laurie Green was taking a lunch break from her job with the Mat-Su Borough School District when she captured the photo that took best of show in this year's Alaska Wild 2006 competition. Green knew waxwings were on the move and went outside to see if she could spot a few. Sure enough, she did. Quickly pulling her Nikon D2H digital camera to her eyes, she adjusted its settings, aimed and shot a few pictures of the birds in flight. "I was so thankful to be behind the camera for that to occur," she said of her winning photo, which shows a gracefully angled waxwing in flight, plucking a bright red berr…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: A healthy commute Bike to Work Day isn't just Friday; for some, it's every day By KYLE HOPKINS and ROSEMARY SHINOHARA Anchorage Daily News Published: May 18, 2006 Last Modified: May 18, 2006 at 02:43 AM The hills on Judy Abrahams' roughly 20-mile commute hurt -- particularly on the ride home when she cycles up the punishing Hillside. But $3-a-gallon gasoline hurts too. And sipping coffee while listening to talk radio may get your blood pumping for the workday, but not as much as careening down the Coastal Trail. The bicycle commuting season is here and bike shops and some employers say more Anchorage workers are making the same choice Abrahams made this week, when she left her pickup at home and pedaled to work for the first time. Conoco Phillips, where Abrahams works as a mechanical engin…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: More salmon being caught in waters of North Slope By WESLEY LOY Anchorage Daily News Published: May 21, 2006 Last Modified: May 21, 2006 at 05:11 AM Click to enlarge Alaskans are heading into another summer of salmon plenty, and by the time commercial fishermen haul in their last gillnet and sportsmen cast their final hook, the catch is expected to top 160 million fish. Nearly all will be taken along the state's historic salmon belt, the southern coastline from Ketchikan to Cordova to Kenai to Kodiak to Bristol Bay. In recent years, however, salmon have seemed to turn up more and more in an odd place -- the Arctic Ocean, which laps the top of the state. Just ask Dora Nukapigak, cultural coordinator for the North Slope village of Nuiqsut. She's sure villagers who have long caught fish such …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Bicycling may be activity to change your life CRAIG MEDRED OUTDOORS Published: May 21, 2006 Last Modified: May 22, 2006 at 01:54 AM WILLOW -- The long line of people on bikes trying to better themselves stretched north along the George Parks Highway for tens of miles on Saturday. Way out front were the kind of people you think of when someone mentions long-distance cyclists. You know, the whittled ones with the bony frames and the lean faces, like Lance Armstrong and his Tour de France gang. As whippet-thin young neighbor Will Murphy is prone to remind on a regular basis through actions, not words, cycling prowess is all about power-to-weight ratios. Skinny little guys need but a fraction of the power of even slightly overweight big guys to make the contraption go, particularly up hills. T…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Lone hiker conquers fear and Alaska 1,500 MILES: In dead of winter, man treks from Glennallen to Kaktovik. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: May 21, 2006 Last Modified: May 21, 2006 at 04:31 AM The first step would prove to be the toughest of Italian Ario Sciolari's winter-long solo trek across about 1,500 miles of frozen Alaska. Back in civilization now, the 39-year-old climber admits that when his journey began near Glennallen in December, he was so fearful his knees shook. "I was so scared in the beginning," he said. "I did not know what to expect."' Had it not been for a commitment to walk all the way to Kaktovik on the North Slope of the Brooks Range to highlight the threat posed to the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge by oil drilling, the mountain guide confesses he migh…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Satellite sleuths are playing on a global game board Geocachers use GPS to find treasure troves in a high-tech hunt By KATIE PESZNECKER Anchorage Daily News Published: May 22, 2006 Last Modified: May 22, 2006 at 01:07 AM Boots crunched over old snow, thudded against wood planks of a derelict military train bed, then sank into mud as the hikers ascended a final incline. "What we're going to see when we get up here is breathtaking," said Wes Skinner, with a backward glance at the dozen or so following him into the depths of Kincaid Park. "Seriously," Skinner said. "It's a million-dollar view." But no one looked at the view. They did not pause to take in the bare spruce trees, or the bald eagle overhead, or the flock of geese to the north. Instead, the hikers stared at cell phone-sized gadget…
